text:
The ability to associate images with descriptive natural language is a hallmark of computer vision and a fundamental requirement for applications like sentence-based image search. In this paper, we propose framing sentence-based image annotation as a ranking task. By treating the problem as one of retrieval rather than generation, we can assess how well a system maps visual content to semantic meaning without the confounding variables of linguistic ﬂuency.

To support this research, we introduce a new benchmark collection of 8,000 images, each paired with five distinct, crowd-sourced captions that describe salient entities and events. We evaluate several systems on this dataset, demonstrating that models using features obtained with minimal supervision can perform remarkably well. Our findings highlight two critical factors for success: training on multiple captions per image and incorporating both syntactic (word-order) and semantic features into the models.

Furthermore, we conduct an extensive comparison of human and automatic evaluation metrics. We propose strategies for collecting human relevance judgments at scale and at low cost, allowing us to augment our dataset with additional annotations. Our analysis reveals that ranking-based metrics—which consider an entire list of results—are significantly more robust than those based on a single top response. Our results suggest that, within this ranking framework, the evaluation of image description systems can be effectively automated.
